summary: Move BSON C++ code to bson-ext npm module and make optional dependency in bson package
description: Move BSON C++ code to bson-ext npm module and make optional dependency in bson package to avoid all the confusion caused by windows users.

summary: Documentation error for GridStore
Expose GridStore.currentChunk.chunkNumber
description:
Exposing {{GridStore.currentChunk.chunkNumber}} as a read only property would be useful. 

When streaming a file I need to know when I get the the first chunk and hence use {{this.currentChunk.chunkNumber}} for this. Digging into the internals  of GridStore is not ideal.

summary: $elemMatch in projection combined with another field: sockets closed
description:
Upgrading from 1.4.34 to 2.0.25 I got this problem.

When querying a single element within an array I'm using $elemMath in the fields projection to grab only that element:

filter (get the right document): 
{ someArrayField: { $elemMatch: { _id: ObjectID(...) } } }

options (get only the wanted object from the array): 
{ fields: { someArrayField: { $elemMatch: { _id: ObjectID(...) } }, someOtherField: 0 } }

With 1.4.34: no problem. 
Within the console: no problem.
With 2.0.25: MongoError: "[port] sockets closed". 
Removing "someOtherField: 0" from the projection it works in 2.0.25 too. 

Note: because $elemMatch is used for a positive match (like x: 1) "someOtherField: 0" appeared to be unnecessary but it was generically added by my code. So practically this was easy for me to resolve.

But the existance of the unnecessary "someOtherField: 0" shouldn't result in an error(?)

summary: Multiple $pullAll not working
updateOne ignores the *write concern* option
description: By reading this document: http://mongodb.github.io/node-mongodb-native/2.0/tutorials/crud_operations/, I might come to the conclusion that `updateOne` honors the write concern option. Yet it seems it always requires a callback (see attached `updateOne-w0.js`).

summary: GridFS: Error: EIO, i/o error
NodeJS diver ignores max pool size
description:
Hello,

We're using version 2.0.21 of the MongoDB NodeJS driver.   In our environment it appears to ignore the maximum pool size.  We are using the following options:

"replicaSet=lap&maxPoolSize=100&bufferMaxEntries=2&maxTimeMS=3000&connectTimeoutMS=10000&autoReconnect=true&retryMilliSeconds=1000"

summary: cursor routine calls block for a long time with 2.x driver against 3.0.1 mongodb
description: Whenever I make a call to toarray, batchsize, or next on the cursor returned from find, the driver blocks for a VERY long time before returning via the call back.

summary: GridStoreStream.write returns false
description:
The last example in the [Streams|http://mongodb.github.io/node-mongodb-native/2.0/tutorials/streams/] tutorial, "Streaming a File into GridFS" doesn't work.

Stream piping doesn't work when writing to a GridStoreStream because [GridStoreStream.prototype.write|https://github.com/mongodb/node-mongodb-native/blob/2.0/lib/gridfs/grid_store.js#L1400] always returns false. As you can see from the node stream docs from [writable.write|https://nodejs.org/api/stream.html#stream_writable_write_chunk_encoding_callback]:
{quote}
The return value indicates if you should continue writing right now. If the data had to be buffered internally, then it will return false. Otherwise, it will return true.

This return value is strictly advisory. You MAY continue to write, even if it returns false. However, writes will be buffered in memory, so it is best not to do this excessively. Instead, wait for the drain event before writing more data.
{quote}

In my opinion, GridStoreStream.write should always return "true", but I understand that since the chunk is technically being buffered into memory "false" is actually the correct return value. That way the GridStoreStream instance passes the chunk along to self.gs.write that actually saves the chunk asynchronously. When it's done, GridStoreStream.write emits the 'drain' event which should properly signal stream.pipe to write another chunk but this is where another issue arrises that I can't get my head around: the drain event always gets triggered before GridStoreStream.write returns false. So now when stream.pipe gets a false response from GridStoreStream.write, it pauses the readstream and waits for the writestream to emit 'drain' - only the GridStoreStream has already emitted it's last 'drain' event already so stream.pipe just sits there waiting for something that will never happen.

*So here's my proposed solution:*
It seems like the internal function [writeBuffer|https://github.com/mongodb/node-mongodb-native/blob/2.0/lib/gridfs/grid_store.js#L859] is async only when it has to close "self". This is actually a good thing b/c stream.pipe doesn't care what your writestream.write() returns if it has nothing else to write to it. So incorrectly returning "true" on the last write seems ok. That means that we can always return true from GridStoreStream.write except for when you've got to open the underlying "self.gs" which is async. So I think this function should return true if the underlying gridstore is open else return false and emit 'drain' when it finishes asynchronously opening the gridstore.

*A possible fallback* is to leave the current implementation of always returning false from GridStoreStream.write but use process.nextTick before each self.emit('drain') to ensure GridStoreStream.write returns false first and drain is actually expecting our 'drain' event.

summary: GridStoreStream._read() doesn't check GridStore.read() error
description:
In GridStoreStream._read() when calling GridStore.read() the returned error is ignored:
https://github.com/mongodb/node-mongodb-native/blob/bf4f971862085eadcefb07a8f207049bf3888d0d/lib/gridfs/grid_store.js#L1374

It should be properly handled.

summary: GridStore id not optional
Improper handling of '@' character in password
description:
When provided a url where the password contains an `@` character, the Node driver crashes. For example, the url

```
mongodb://user:p@ssword@localhost:27017/mydb
```

causes the driver to crash with the following error:

```
Error: getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND ssword
    at errnoException (dns.js:44:10)
    at GetAddrInfoReqWrap.onlookup [as oncomplete] (dns.js:94:26)
```

This is because `lib/url_parser.js` assumes that the first occurrence of the `@` character is the delimiter between username:password and host:port.

The MongoDB npm package fails to build on Heroku when using io.js 2.0.0. 

I've attached the deploy logs, some of the logs may be related to other npm packages.

summary: possible memory leak, too many listeners added
description:

emitting "too many listeners" node warnings.  There needs to be a way of
removing the listeners that are no longer needed.

The context is a server where each request first selects the db to
access the correct collections.  There are many dbs and many
concurrent requests.

mongoClient = require('mongodb').MongoClient;

mongoClient.connect("mongodb://localhost", function(err, db) {
  for (i=0; i<10; i++) {

});

summary: Auth failure using Node 0.8.28, MongoDB 3.0.2 & mongodb-node-native 1.4.35
Reusing same options object when calling db.authenticate() with no authMech fails
description: See PR on Github: https://github.com/mongodb/node-mongodb-native/pull/1274 for details

summary: Tripping on error "no chunks found for file, possibly corrupt" when there is no error
description:
I use the mongo native driver in conjunction with the gridfs-stream package.  I use the default values for reading a file from my mongo database, so the chunksize is the default 255K and the buffer size is equal to the chunksize.

I have a problem with retreiveing the last chunk from the file when the filesize happens to be perfectly aligned with the chunk size.  In my instance I have a file that is 1345812480 bytes long, which is exactly 5154 chunks.  When i stream the file out, I get all but the very last chunk.  And it ends with an error 

{ [MongoError: no chunks found for file, possibly corrupt]
  name: 'MongoError',
  message: 'no chunks found for file, possibly corrupt' }

Well, looking at it, the code for .read() in grid_store.js seems awfully complicated and ends up reading data from the database unnecessarily, esp at the end when the buffer size and the chunk size land evenly with the end of the file.  The callback at the end of the function never gets called for the situation where the last buffer is exactly the size of a chunk, instead it errors out.

summary: db.close immediately executes its callback
Single connection per connection object for an arbiter
description: Arbiters should only need a single connection for monitoring, not many.

summary: Reconnect counter left permanently reduced even after successful reconnect
description:
Each time the driver attempts to reconnect, the driver ticks down the number of available reconnect tries. When it successfully reconnects that number is left permanently declined. This pull request makes it so that when a successful reconnect occurs, the number is increased back to it's allowed limit.

Pull request at github: https://github.com/christkv/mongodb-core/pull/23

summary: insert not running callback when database doesn't exist, but still works
Error in callback functions write operation acknowledgment is disabled
findAndModify & dropIndex queries don't use primary server with replicaset
description:
Hi,
On a node project that use a replicaset, when I call findAndModify or dropIndex methods, there is often an error "not master" because requests are executed on a secondary.
I think these kind of method should always use primary (et least for writing part).
To bypass that, I need to add readPreference primary on all call to these functions, and it's not very cool ;)
Thanks.

summary: Cannot db.authenticate with Multiple mongos in Seed URI (SCRAM-SHA-1)
Cursor.next doesn't behave as deprecated Cursor.nextObject for tailable cursors
WiredTiger memory leak
.limit return more than limited value
Cursor.prototype.each calls _each(this, callback) - callback is undefined
description:
As .each is marked deprecated, I used .forEach. That gave me the following warning:

ReferenceError: callback is not defined

Looking into cursor.js, I discovered that Cursor.prototype.each is calling _each, passing in a callback function that is not defined in that function and is a breaking change.

js-bson: Failed to load c++ bson extension, using pure JS version

summary: Change the input arguments for Buffer.write()
Multiple updates cause overwrite?
description:
Apologies if I'm just doing something stupid, but I've been building an application which includes contacts, and when a user accepts a contact, it should remove from "pending" and "sent" contacts, then add to "contacts".

The following Gist describes the issue with example documents: https://gist.github.com/Someguy123/8e054a70f6eba000ddb7

The first update removes the contact and the owner from each-others pending/sent list, the second and third update add the owner to the contact's contact list and vice versa.

The issue is, the final result appears to be as if the removal never happened, though the removal query works perfectly fine by itself. I don't know if this is a problem with MongoDB itself (or if it's intended), or if it's an issue with the driver, so I hope someone can at least clarify this for me, and provide a solution in some form.

summary: maxPoolSize is treated as absolute
description:
Specifying maxPoolSize in Node causes that number of connections to be established immediately upon first connect (no actual database operations are required) which may include authenticating all of them. The "connect" call does not trigger the callback until all maxPoolSize connections have been established. If any one of these fails, the entire connect call fails.

From the server POV, specifying a maxPoolSize of 200:
{noformat}
(198 connections removed)
...
2015-07-03T11:29:51.845+1000 I NETWORK  [initandlisten] connection accepted from 127.0.0.1:51209 #202 (199 connections now open)
2015-07-03T11:29:51.846+1000 I NETWORK  [initandlisten] connection accepted from 127.0.0.1:51200 #203 (200 connections now open)
{noformat}

And since auth was enabled, this was followed by nearly 10 seconds of authenticating:
{noformat}
(198 auths removed)
...
2015-07-03T11:29:59.476+1000 I ACCESS   [conn203] Successfully authenticated as principal admin on admin
2015-07-03T11:29:59.476+1000 I ACCESS   [conn202] Successfully authenticated as principal admin on admin
{noformat}

Node test client console log I think is self explanatory:
{noformat}
URI: mongodb://admin:password@localhost:27017/admin?maxPoolSize=200
connect @ Fri Jul 03 2015 11:29:50 GMT+1000 (AUS Eastern Standard Time)
cb_connected @ Fri Jul 03 2015 11:30:00 GMT+1000 (AUS Eastern Standard Time)
{noformat}

This can have interesting side-effects; if the pool is large (500 is not uncommon) and a mongos is in use in close proximity, then the connections will be received in a very short space of time - in my tests, I found that a pool size of 600 nearly always failed to connect when the mongod/mongos was co-located - the error was ECONNREFUSED. This error occurs when the receiver is unable to process the incoming connection requests fast enough to maintain empty socket accept buffers. With a pool size of 1000 I never managed to connect at all. With a remote located server the problem went away - presumably because the network itself introduced just enough latency to allow the server to keep up.

summary: `useLegacyOps` option for bulk operations mentionned in documentation examples doesn't exist
description:
http://mongodb.github.io/node-mongodb-native/2.0/api/Collection.html

In the bulk operation initialization examples a `useLegacyOps` option is used, but not documented.
grep on mongodb and mongodb-core doesn't list any result: the example is probably a wrong copy/past from 1.4.x.

summary: Collection - Default options when using promiseLibrary
description:
Affects the following Collection methods ...
- findOneAndDelete
- findOneAndReplace
- findOneAndUpdate

When calling these methods with no options and a callback, options is defaulted to {}. When using no options and a promiseLibrary, options are left as undefined. This causes an error in the self.findAndModify( ... ) call (cannot read property 'sort' of undefined).

summary: Accidental repeat of hostname in seed list multiplies total connections persistently
description:
Given the following connection-string:
{code}
mongodb://admin:password@localhost:27101,localhost:27101,localhost:27101,localhost:27101,localhost:27101/admin?maxPoolSize=200
{code}
Obviously, there is an error in this string but it is neither detected nor corrected by the Node driver.

The 3 secondaries received 200 connections.
The primary member received 1000 connections - the majority of which appeared to authenticate.. all of which remained connected over 20 minutes later.

The results from {{isMaster}} should be authoritative, not augmentative.

summary: Driver does not recover when replicaset master crashes
description:
Run the script attached on a secondary node from the attached config and then crash the master.

Expected output is "Insert successfully" approx. every second.

Actual output is "Insert successfully" until the master crashes, then it hangs indefinitely.

summary: Query fails to find records that have a 'result' property with an array value
Collection's bulkWrite method fails when it used as a promise
options.promiseLibrary not passed from Db to Collection in createCollection()
